one_line_takeaway: CloudWeb shows that a fully input-space, deployment-time-unmodified atmospheric (cloud/haze) perturbation to a remote-sensing query image can hijack CLIP-style multimodal RAG retrieval toward attacker-chosen weather evidence (W@5 0.71%->43.29% on GeoRSCLIP) and that this evidence corruption measurably propagates into downstream VLM hallucinations, exposing a retrieval-stage vulnerability distinct from corpus-poisoning or classifier-attack threat models.
key_finding: On GeoRSCLIP ViT-B/32, optimized CloudWeb raises Weather@5 from 0.71% (clean) to 43.29%, and Weather@1 from 0.57% to 29.14%; similar gains hold across all five retrievers (e.g., OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14 reaches W@5=39.29%, RemoteCLIP reaches W@5=8.57%). Crucially, generic retrieval disruption does not imply targeted hijacking: Gaussian Blur and Random Noise Cloud reach high T@5 (near-saturation, e.g. 99%+ on GeoRSCLIP) but far lower W@5 than CloudWeb, and Fixed CloudWeb (same rendering family, no retrieval-oriented optimization) also underperforms the optimized variant -- showing that cloud/haze appearance alone is insufficient and the retrieval-oriented loss is what drives targeted hijacking. Downstream, on the strong303 subset, the effect propagates to generation: Qwen2.5-VL shows the largest shift (172 newly induced weather responses, G-ASR=56.77%, WHR=61.39%), with LLaVA-1.6 (121 NW, WHR=44.55%) and InstructBLIP (107 NW, WHR=38.28%) also strongly affected, versus near-zero effect for Gaussian Blur/Brightness Haze/Random Noise Cloud baselines. Loss ablation shows removing Ltar causes the largest degradation (W@5 drops from 40.86% to 38.00%), and removing Lrank also hurts, while Lnat/Larea act mainly as naturalness/coverage regularizers rather than drivers of attack strength. The attack remains robust post-processing: W@5 stays in the 37.43%-43.14% range under JPEG compression, resizing, and mild Gaussian blur (JPEG Q=30 even raises W@5 to 43.14%), attributed to the attack being carried by low-frequency atmospheric structure rather than fragile high-frequency pixel noise. Per-dataset breakdown (Appendix D) shows strong dataset dependence: RSVQA-LR is most vulnerable (W@5 up to 83.0% on GeoRSCLIP, 90.0% on OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14), while RSIVQA-UCM/RSIVQA-Sydney are comparatively resistant.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CloudWeb, an input-space adversarial attack against the retrieval stage of remote-sensing multimodal RAG systems. Rather than poisoning the retrieval corpus/database (the dominant prior threat model in RAG attack literature) or attacking downstream classifier/VLM predictions, CloudWeb perturbs only the query image at inference time by overlaying a low-dimensional, parameterized cloud/haze pattern (severity, opacity, haze strength, edge softness, texture seed) that is optimized with a retrieval-oriented loss so that the CLIP-style image embedding is pulled toward target 'atmospheric evidence' text embeddings and pushed away from source-scene evidence, with an added margin-based rank-separation term and naturalness/coverage regularizers to keep the perturbation visually plausible. The perturbation is optimized via differential evolution with local refinement (population=8, iterations=8, local refinement steps=2) rather than gradient descent, and the retriever, generator, and vector database are left completely unmodified at deployment -- only the query image changes.
threat_model: Input-space, black-box-at-deployment attacker who modifies only the query image submitted to a frozen remote-sensing multimodal RAG pipeline (CLIP-style image encoder -> cosine-similarity top-k retrieval over a fixed text-evidence corpus -> VLM generator conditioned on retrieved evidence). The attacker has no access to the retriever, generator, corpus, or vector database at deployment time, but is assumed to have offline access to the retriever's image/text encoders to pre-compute source-scene and target-atmospheric text embeddings and to optimize the perturbation parameters before the attack is deployed. The attack explicitly does not poison the retrieval corpus/memory (contrasted against PoisonedRAG, AgentPoison, knowledge-poisoning-style RAG attacks) and does not target a classifier's or VLM's output logits directly (contrasted against AdvCloud/AdvHaze-style perception attacks); instead the target is the intermediate evidence-retrieval stage itself.
limitations: The paper's own scope caveats: (1) the downstream generation results (Table 2) are explicitly a conditional-propagation measurement on strong303 -- a subset pre-selected for successful GeoRSCLIP retrieval-stage hijacking -- not an end-to-end attack success rate over the full 700-query benchmark, so the headline generation numbers should not be read as unconditional real-world success rates. (2) The paper explicitly declines to compare against unconstrained pixel/patch adversarial attacks, arguing they optimize a different (non-plausibility-constrained) perturbation class, and defers that comparison to future work as an empirical upper bound -- meaning the paper cannot claim CloudWeb is competitive with or superior to unconstrained attacks in raw effectiveness, only that it is more visually plausible. (3) Optimization requires offline white-box access to the retriever's image/text encoders to compute source/target embeddings before deployment; the paper does not test true black-box transfer to retrievers unseen during optimization. (4) The LLM-based judge (a single model, google/gemma-4-31b-it) is used for all generation-stage ASR/WHR/G-ASR metrics, introducing judge-model dependency/bias not cross-validated against a second judge or human raters within the visible text. (5) The paper states in its own ablation discussion that 'visual plausibility here means ambiguity, not imperceptibility' and that artifacts can remain visible (deferred to Appendix E), i.e., the attack is not claimed to be imperceptible, only plausible/natural-looking as weather rather than as adversarial noise.

datasets_used: strong303 (303-case subset for downstream generation evaluation)
quantitative_results: Downstream generation on strong303: Qwen2.5-VL NW=172, G-ASR=56.77%, ASR=61.39%, WHR=61.39%
Post-processing robustness: W@5 remains 37.43%-43.14% under JPEG compression, resizing, and mild Gaussian blur (JPEG Q=30 raises W@5 to 43.14%)
Loss ablation: removing Ltar reduces W@5 from 40.86% to 38.00%
Perturbation-component ablation: lower opacity/severity reduces W@5 to 8.00% and 11.71% respectively
Opacity-severity interaction: best cell at opacity 0.9, severity 0.85 across all five retrievers; OpenAI CLIP-L/14 reaches strongest fixed-parameter W@5 of 26.00%
Per-dataset Weather@5: RSVQA-LR most vulnerable (83.0% GeoRSCLIP, 90.0% OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14); RSIVQA-UCM/RSIVQA-Sydney noticeably more resistant
baselines_compared: Fixed CloudWeb (same atmospheric rendering family without retrieval-oriented optimization)
